i-seed00001.title: Platform v2.0 Migration
i-seed00001.description:
Platform v2.0 Migration

Migrate the entire platform to v2.0 architecture. This is a large initiative covering authentication, database, API changes, and real-time collaboration.

## Goals
- Modernize authentication with OAuth2
- Migrate database schema for new entity model
- Implement API rate limiting
- Build real-time collaboration engine
- Deploy GraphQL federation gateway

## Timeline
Targeting completion by end of Q2.
i-seed00001.creator: alice

i-seed00020.title: Implement concurrent refresh queue with mutex
i-seed00020.description:
Implement concurrent refresh queue with mutex

Guard the OAuth2 token refresh flow with a per-client mutex so concurrent requests share a single in-flight refresh and don't all hit the IdP simultaneously.

## Approach
- Wrap refresh in a `p-limit`-style queue keyed by client_id
- Pending callers await the resolved token from the in-flight refresh
- Surface refresh errors to all queued callers (not just the first)
i-seed00020.creator: swe

p-seed00001.title: Add OAuth2 provider integration
p-seed00001.description:
Integrates Keycloak as the OAuth2 / OIDC provider. Implements authorization code flow with PKCE, token validation middleware, and user info endpoint.

Key changes:
- New `auth/oauth2.ts` module with provider configuration
- Updated `middleware/auth.ts` to validate JWT tokens
- Added `routes/auth/callback.ts` for OAuth2 callback handling
- New environment variables: `OAUTH2_ISSUER_URL`, `OAUTH2_CLIENT_ID`
p-seed00001.creator: swe

reviews.contents:
Looks good overall. A few questions:
1. Should we add token caching to reduce calls to the OIDC provider?
2. The error handling in the callback route could be more specific.
3. Consider adding a health check endpoint that verifies OIDC provider connectivity.

+// initial skeleton

p-seed00003.title: Add rate limiter middleware
p-seed00003.description: Implements a sliding window rate limiter using Redis. Configurable per-endpoint limits with different tiers for authenticated and anonymous users.
p-seed00003.creator: swe

reviews.contents: The sliding window implementation has a race condition between INCR and EXPIRE. Use a Lua script or MULTI/EXEC to make it atomic. Also, the Redis connection pool isn't being closed on shutdown which causes the memory leak reported in i-seed00006.

p-seed00004.title: Deploy Apollo Federation gateway with Users and Issues subgraphs
p-seed00004.description:
Sets up the GraphQL federation gateway and implements the first two subgraphs.

Key changes:
- Apollo Federation v2 gateway configuration
- Users subgraph: profile queries, team membership, auth integration
- Issues subgraph: CRUD operations, dependency graph resolution
- Dataloader integration for N+1 prevention
p-seed00004.diff:
diff --git a/src/gateway/config.ts b/src/gateway/config.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..111aaa
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/gateway/config.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,15 @@
+import { ApolloGateway } from '@apollo/gateway';
+
+export const gateway = new ApolloGateway({
+  supergraphSdl: new IntrospectAndCompose({
+    subgraphs: [
+      { name: 'users', url: process.env.USERS_SUBGRAPH_URL },
+      { name: 'issues', url: process.env.ISSUES_SUBGRAPH_URL },
+    ],
+  }),

p-seed00006.title: Fix SSE broadcaster memory leak on client disconnect
p-seed00006.description:
Fixes unbounded memory growth in the SSE event broadcaster caused by accumulated listeners from disconnected clients.

Root cause: cleanup callback not registered on request AbortSignal.
p-seed00006.creator: swe

p-seed00007.title: Add GIN index on issues.description for full-text search
p-seed00007.description:
Adds a GIN index on the issues.description column using `to_tsvector('english', description)` to accelerate the full-text search endpoint.

Migration is non-blocking (`CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`); rollback path included.
p-seed00007.diff:
diff --git a/migrations/0042_issues_description_gin.sql b/migrations/0042_issues_description_gin.sql
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..ccc2222
--- /dev/null
+++ b/migrations/0042_issues_description_gin.sql
@@ -0,0 +1,6 @@
+-- 0042_issues_description_gin
+CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS issues_description_gin
+  ON issues USING GIN (to_tsvector('english', description));
+
+-- rollback:
+-- DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS issues_description_gin;
p-seed00007.creator: swe

p-seed00008.title: WebSocket ConnectionManager with JWT auth and heartbeat
p-seed00008.description:
Implements the core `ConnectionManager` class: handles connect/disconnect, JWT auth on handshake, ping/pong heartbeat, and message routing.

Reconnection with exponential backoff is intentionally left for a follow-up patch.
p-seed00008.creator: swe

reviews.contents: Good first cut. Two notes: (1) consider moving `startHeartbeat` interval to a class constant; (2) the connection map is keyed by user_id — what's the plan for multiple concurrent connections from one user?

p-seed00009.title: Distributed lock for session cleanup worker (Redis SETNX)
p-seed00009.description: Replaces the racy `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` approach with a per-session Redis SETNX lock keyed by session_id. Locks auto-expire after 30s so a crashed worker can't strand a session.
p-seed00009.creator: swe

p-seed00010.title: Per-client refresh queue mutex for OAuth2 token refresh
p-seed00010.description:
Wraps the OAuth2 token refresh flow with a per-client mutex so concurrent callers share a single in-flight refresh. Resolves the thundering-herd issue against the IdP.

Known issue: under burst load (>500 simultaneous callers) the queue can deadlock when an in-flight refresh rejects. Tracking in i-seed00020.
p-seed00010.creator: swe

reviews.contents: The queue itself is clean, but the deadlock under burst load needs to be fixed before merge. Specifically, when the in-flight promise rejects, queued callers should also see the rejection — right now if `inflight.delete` runs before they `.then()`, the next call sees no in-flight and starts another refresh, which is the bug.

failure.name: burst-load-test
failure.summary: burst-load test reproduces deadlock at 1k concurrent callers (timeout 30s)

d-seed00001.title: ADR-001: OAuth2 Migration Strategy
d-seed00001.body_markdown:
# ADR-001: OAuth2 Migration Strategy

## Status
Accepted

## Context
Our current session-based authentication does not scale well across multiple services and lacks standardized token exchange capabilities needed for our microservices migration.

## Decision
We will migrate to OAuth2 / OpenID Connect using Keycloak as the identity provider.

### Key decisions:
1. **Provider**: Keycloak (self-hosted) for maximum control
2. **Flow**: Authorization Code with PKCE for web apps
3. **Token format**: JWT with RS256 signing
4. **Session migration**: Gradual rollout with feature flags

## Consequences
- Users will need to re-authenticate once during migration
- All services must validate JWT tokens independently
- Adds operational complexity of managing Keycloak

d-seed00002.title: API Reference Guide v2.0
d-seed00002.body_markdown:
# API Reference Guide v2.0

## Base URL
`https://api.acme.dev/v2`

## Authentication
All requests require a Bearer token:
```
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
```

## Endpoints

### Issues
| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| GET | /v1/issues | List all issues |
| POST | /v1/issues | Create a new issue |
| GET | /v1/issues/:id | Get issue details |
| PUT | /v1/issues/:id | Update an issue |
| DELETE | /v1/issues/:id | Delete an issue |

### Jobs
| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| GET | /v1/jobs | List all jobs |
| POST | /v1/jobs | Create a new job |
| GET | /v1/jobs/:id | Get job details |

## Rate Limiting
All endpoints are rate-limited. Check the `X-RateLimit-Remaining` header.

d-seed00003.title: Developer Onboarding Guide
d-seed00003.body_markdown:
# Developer Onboarding Guide

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites
- Node.js 20+
- Docker and Docker Compose
- Access to the GitHub organization

### Setup
1. Clone the repository: `git clone https://github.com/acme/web-app.git`
2. Install dependencies: `pnpm install`
3. Copy config: `cp .env.example .env`
4. Start services: `docker compose up -d`
5. Run migrations: `pnpm migrate`
6. Start dev server: `pnpm dev`

### Project Structure
```
src/
  auth/       - Authentication modules
  middleware/ - Express middleware
  routes/     - API route handlers
  models/     - Database models
  utils/      - Shared utilities
```

## Common Tasks
- **Run tests**: `pnpm test`
- **Lint**: `pnpm lint`
- **Build**: `pnpm build`

d-seed00004.title: ADR-002: Real-time Collaboration Architecture
d-seed00004.body_markdown:
# ADR-002: Real-time Collaboration Architecture

## Status
Accepted

## Context
We need to support real-time collaboration features including presence tracking, concurrent editing, and live activity feeds.

## Decision
Event-driven architecture using:
1. **WebSocket Gateway** for client connections and message routing
2. **Redis Streams** for inter-service event passing
3. **Yjs CRDTs** for conflict-free concurrent document editing
4. **PostgreSQL** for persistence

### Key decisions:
- CRDTs over OT (simpler, no central coordination)
- Redis Streams over Kafka (lower overhead for our scale)
- Single multiplexed WebSocket connection per client

## Consequences
- All services must publish events to Redis Streams
- WebSocket connections are stateful, complicating horizontal scaling
- Need Redis Cluster for high availability

d-seed00006.title: Per-project issue statuses rollout plan
d-seed00006.body_markdown:
# Per-project issue statuses rollout plan

The per-project issue-status design lets each project declare its own status taxonomy (e.g. `triage`, `in-review`, `shipped`) on top of the platform-wide `open` / `in-progress` / `closed` set. This doc tracks the rollout.

## Phases

1. Add `project_statuses` column to the issue table (migration only, no API change).
2. Surface custom statuses on the issue detail rail.
3. Filter chips on the issue list page.
4. Bulk re-statusing tools for migrations between schemas.

d-sys-prompt.title: System prompt
d-sys-prompt.body_markdown:
You are a Hydra agent. The sections below cover the generic Hydra-usage boilerplate every named agent inherits: tools, environment, document-store sync, reference conventions, feedback handling, the required first step, and session-lifecycle rules.

## Tools

`hydra issues`, `hydra patches`, `hydra documents`. Run `hydra <command> --help` for syntax.

## Environment

Your issue id is in `$HYDRA_ISSUE_ID`. (Conversation-bound agents use `$HYDRA_CONVERSATION_ID` instead.)

## Document store

Documents are synced to `$HYDRA_DOCUMENTS_DIR` before your session starts. Prefer standard filesystem tools for reads and writes. If you edit files there you MUST push them back with `hydra documents push`.

## Referencing Hydra objects

Use double-bracket form: `[[i-abcd12]]`, `[[p-xxxxxx]]`, `[[d-yyyyyy]]`.

## Required first step

Run `hydra graph log "$HYDRA_ISSUE_ID | scope" --since -7d --verbosity 2` to stream object-level updates across your issue and its connected sub-graph.

## Session lifecycle

Multiple agents may pick up an issue, so leave enough info in the issue tracker (progress field, status) for the next agent to continue. NEVER poll, sleep-loop, or repeatedly check child status.

d-proj-default-prompt.title: Default project prompt
d-proj-default-prompt.body_markdown:
## Project workflow: Default

This is the default Hydra workflow for issues that don't belong to a bespoke project. It reproduces the original PM-triages / SWE-implements / reviewer-reviews / merger-merges pipeline.

### Roles and routing

- **PM** agents triage `open` issues, investigate, and dispatch SWE-assigned children via the `child-of` dependency type.
- **SWE** agents implement on `in-progress` issues and create child `review-request` issues to hand patches to the reviewer.
- **Reviewer** agents pick up the `review-request` child, scan the patch, post a verdict on the review-request issue, and close themselves.
- **Merger** agents merge approved patches once review gating clears.

### Status progression

`open` → `in-progress` → `closed`. `dropped` and `failed` are terminal states reached when work is cancelled or hits a blocker. Terminal statuses do not spawn agent sessions.

d-proj-default-open.title: Default project: open status prompt
d-proj-default-open.body_markdown:
## Status: open

A new issue, not yet picked up. The action depends on which agent the spawn dispatcher routed onto it.

### If you are PM

You are triaging this issue. Gather context with `hydra graph log`, list playbooks and repos, and clone implicated repos.

**Task breakdown.** Produce 1–6 tasks, each one PR-sized. Each task description must include: goal and user-visible outcome, scope (in / out), key files to touch, acceptance criteria and required tests, and dependencies.

Create tasks via `hydra issues create` with `--deps child-of:$HYDRA_ISSUE_ID`. Assign to `swe` unless the issue specifies a different assignee.

### If you are SWE

Set status to `in-progress` and proceed — the `in-progress` status prompt covers the active-work commands.

### If you are reviewer

This is a `review-request` issue assigned to you. Read the attached patch, post a verdict via `hydra patches review`, then close the review-request issue.

d-proj-default-inprog.title: Default project: in-progress status prompt
d-proj-default-inprog.body_markdown:
## Status: in-progress

The issue is being actively worked. SWE is the usual occupant; the action depends on whether the issue already has patches.

### Implementation work — no patches yet

1. Implement the change. You are already on a branch for this issue — commit your changes there.
2. Submit via `hydra patches create`, assigning to the issue's `creator`.

### Implementation work — patches exist

- **Merged**: task may be done. Create follow-ups as independent items.
- **ChangesRequested**: address comments, then reopen with `hydra patches update <PATCH_ID> --status Open`.
- **Open with approved review**: merge with `hydra patches merge <patch-id>`.
- **Closed**: rework the patch and resubmit a new one.

### Handling `merge_blocked` errors

Dispatch on `blocked_at_layer`. For `reviews`: file review-request issues. For `mergers`: file ONE merge-request issue and end the session.

Once all needed changes are merged and follow-ups are complete, set status to `closed`.

d-proj-engv2-prompt.title: engineering-v2 project prompt
d-proj-engv2-prompt.body_markdown:
## Project workflow: engineering-v2

A columnar pipeline that routes work through six declared statuses: `inbox` → `backlog` → `in-development` → `in-review` → `pending-release`. `pending` is a holding state outside the main flow.

### Roles and routing

Each non-terminal status carries an `on_enter` automation that reassigns the issue to a specific agent when the issue transitions into it:

- `backlog` → `pm` (triage and breakdown)
- `in-development` → `swe` (implementation; may produce one or more patches)
- `in-review` → `reviewer` (form-driven review via `/forms/review.yaml`; verdict flips back to `in-development` or forward to `pending-release`)

### Same-issue review hand-off

Unlike the default pipeline, **no child `review-request` issue is created**. SWE transitions the same issue from `in-development` to `in-review`, and reviewer picks up the same issue id. This keeps activity, patches, and progress notes on one timeline.

### Dependency semantics

- `pending-release` is terminal for dependency semantics (`unblocks_parents = unblocks_dependents = true`) but does **not** cascade to children.
- No status has `cascades_to_children = true`.

d-proj-engv2-backlog.title: engineering-v2: backlog status prompt
d-proj-engv2-backlog.body_markdown:
## Status: backlog

A freshly triaged issue waiting for breakdown. The `on_enter` automation reassigned the issue to `pm` — that's you.

### Your job

1. Read the issue description and check `hydra graph log` for related work.
2. Decide if the issue needs breakdown into child tasks, or can be picked up directly as a single PR.
3. If breaking down, file children via `hydra issues create ... --deps child-of:$HYDRA_ISSUE_ID --project engineering-v2`. Children default to `inbox`, then move to `backlog` for triage.
4. If picking up directly, transition to `in-development` — the `on_enter` automation routes the issue to `swe`.

Do not file a separate `review-request` child later; in `engineering-v2` review handoff happens via the same-issue `in-development` → `in-review` transition.

d-proj-engv2-indev.title: engineering-v2: in-development status prompt
d-proj-engv2-indev.body_markdown:
## Status: in-development

Active implementation work. The `on_enter` automation reassigned this issue to `swe` — that's you.

### Your job

1. Implement the change on this issue's branch.
2. Submit patches via `hydra patches create`, assigning to the issue's `creator`.
3. Once the patch is up for review, transition the issue from `in-development` → `in-review`. The `on_enter` automation reassigns to `reviewer` and attaches `/forms/review.yaml`.
4. **Do not** file a separate `review-request` child — the same-issue review hand-off is the engineering-v2 convention.

If the reviewer flips the issue back to `in-development` (request_changes), read `issue.feedback`, address the comments, and transition forward to `in-review` again.

d-proj-engv2-inrev.title: engineering-v2: in-review status prompt
d-proj-engv2-inrev.body_markdown:
## Status: in-review

An implementation is ready for review. The `on_enter` automation attached `/forms/review.yaml` and reassigned to `reviewer` — that's you.

### Your job

1. Read the latest patches on the issue via `hydra patches list --issue $HYDRA_ISSUE_ID`.
2. Check the diff for correctness, security, style, and test coverage.
3. Submit the attached form via `hydra issues submit-form`. The form action emits one of two effects:
   - `request_changes`: `Effect::UpdateIssue { status: "in-development", add_comment_from: "review_comment" }` — flips status back and posts an issue comment for SWE.
   - `approve`: `Effect::UpdateIssue { status: "pending-release" }` — terminal-for-dependencies status.

Do not close this issue manually; the form action drives the transition.

reviewer.prompt: You are a code review agent. You review patches for correctness, security, and style.

Here is a brief status update.

The Platform v2.0 migration is in progress: OAuth2 and the database migration are complete; rate limiting, the collaboration engine, and the GraphQL gateway are still open. Three agents are actively working in this area.

t-seed00017.content: Q1 highlights: auth migration, initial DB schema rollout, and the documents UI refresh.

t-seed00011.content: Where are we on the OAuth2 migration? I need to update the steering doc.

OAuth2 migration is mid-flight. The provider integration patch is merged, the scopes/permissions patch is in review, and the token refresh subtask is still open. The migration ADR (d-seed00001) is the canonical reference.
Any blockers on the refresh subtask?
Yes — i-seed00003 is blocked on resolving the concurrent-refresh race condition. There's a draft mutex design in p-seed00010 that addresses it but needs review.

t-seed00018.content: I'm trying to map out the realtime collab work. Can you walk me through what's outstanding?

Two issues drive this: i-seed00013 (WebSocket connection manager) and i-seed00014 (Yjs CRDT integration). The connection manager has a draft patch (p-seed00007) with JWT auth and heartbeat; the Yjs piece has p-seed00005 attaching CRDT to PostgreSQL persistence. ADR-002 (d-seed00004) captures the overall architecture.
Are the CRDT and WebSocket pieces sequenced or independent?
Independent in implementation, but the demo cannot ship without both. ADR-002 calls for a single multiplexed connection per client, so the gateway needs to be live before CRDT messages start flowing.

When can we deprecate the REST endpoints?
Phase 3, per the plan. Targets are not committed yet — depends on client migration.

t-seed00008.content: We're seeing SSE clients drop and memory climb on the broadcaster. Where do we start?

i-seed00016 captures the leak; the fix is staged in p-seed00006 (release listeners on client disconnect). The review issue is i-seed00018. The API reference doc (d-seed00002) has the SSE endpoint contract if you need to verify client behaviour against the spec.
Any chance this is related to the websocket gateway work?
Unrelated — the leak is in the SSE broadcaster's per-client listener registry, not the websocket gateway. Different process.
